worth_it_explanation: Demand tags decide which buyer gets the chance to fill each video or Connected TV (CTV) impression, and in what order, so the way they are prioritized and budgeted controls both fill rate and the price each impression clears at. A tag set up with the wrong priority, or a stale tag left running long after a deal ended, quietly reshapes the auction without anyone deciding it should. The check is a review of the demand tags and partners a publisher already has in FreeWheel, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete configuration the publisher can correct.
intro: In FreeWheel, a publisher fills video and CTV inventory from a mix of direct-sold demand and third-party demand partners, each carried by a tag that competes for the impression at a priority and within a budget the publisher sets. This subcategory is about the demand tags and partners themselves, how they are prioritized, budgeted, and kept current, rather than the inventory packaging or the deal terms negotiated outside the ad server. These checks confirm that the demand tags reflect deliberate priority and budget decisions and that tags which no longer earn their place have been removed.

audit_checks.title: Direct demand and third-party partner tags carry the wrong priority or budget
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Priority decides which demand source gets first call on an impression, and budget caps how much of the inventory a source can take. A direct-sold deal placed below a third-party partner tag, or a partner tag with no budget cap competing freely against guaranteed demand, can starve the higher-value demand of impressions it was sold to fill, so the misconfiguration trades committed revenue for whatever the auction returns.

educate.base:
FreeWheel serves a video or Connected TV (CTV) impression by working through the demand a publisher has configured in a set order. Direct-sold demand, the deals a sales team negotiated with named advertisers, usually sits at a high priority because the publisher committed to deliver it. Third-party demand partners, the programmatic and network tags that fill what direct demand leaves behind, sit below it. Priority is the lever that decides who gets first call on the impression, and budget is the lever that decides how much of the inventory each source is allowed to take before it steps aside.

The two levers have to agree with what the publisher actually sold. A guaranteed direct deal that was promised a fixed number of impressions needs a priority high enough to win them and a budget that matches the commitment. A third-party partner tag that exists to backfill needs a priority below the guaranteed demand and, in many setups, a cap so it cannot crowd out demand the publisher is obligated to deliver. When the levers drift out of agreement, the auction stops reflecting the publisher's commitments. A direct deal placed below a partner tag loses impressions it was sold to fill. A partner tag with no cap competing at the same level as guaranteed demand takes inventory the publisher owed elsewhere.

The tag itself also has to be correct. A third-party demand partner delivers its creative through a video ad tag built to the IAB Tech Lab Video Ad Serving Template (VAST), and programmatic partners transact through the OpenRTB protocol, so a tag pointed at the wrong endpoint, carrying a stale parameter, or set to the wrong format can fail to return demand even when its priority and budget are right.

A few signs point to demand tags whose priority or budget no longer matches the publisher's commitments:
  • A guaranteed direct-sold deal sits at or below the priority of a backfill partner tag
  • A third-party partner tag runs with no budget cap while guaranteed demand is under-delivering
  • The priority order across demand sources does not match the order the publisher would describe if asked which demand should win first
  • A partner tag returns errors or no fill in reporting, which can point to a stale endpoint or a format mismatch rather than thin demand
You may want to confirm that each demand source carries a priority and budget that match what the publisher sold. One approach is to list every direct and partner tag with its priority and budget, then check that ordering against the publisher's own account of which demand should win first.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a streaming or broadcaster property, guaranteed sponsorships and direct-sold campaigns against premium programming carry delivery commitments that the priority order has to protect. You may want to confirm that those guaranteed deals sit above the programmatic backfill partners, and that the backfill tags carry caps so a strong programmatic bid cannot take impressions a sponsorship was sold to fill during a flagship show or live event.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content video property, such as shoppable video or product-review streams, direct deals tied to a retail campaign or a brand partnership often need to land against specific content, so their priority has to clear the general backfill partners. You may want to confirm that the commerce-tied direct demand wins those placements and that an uncapped partner tag is not absorbing the higher-intent inventory the direct deal was meant to fill.

educate.learn_more_note: FreeWheel's detailed configuration documentation for demand-source priority and budget settings is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the priority and budget set on each direct-sold deal and third-party demand partner tag, and check that ordering against what the publisher sold and what reporting shows each source returning.

freewheel_ui.label: FreeWheel console
steps: Open the demand configuration and list every direct-sold deal and third-party demand partner tag that is live against the inventory.
For each demand source, record its priority tier and its budget or impression cap, if one is set.
Order the sources by priority and compare that order against the publisher's own account of which demand should win first, noting any guaranteed deal that sits below a backfill partner.
For each third-party partner tag, confirm the tag endpoint and format are current since a stale Video Ad Serving Template (VAST) tag or wrong format can return no fill regardless of priority.

freewheel_reporting.label: FreeWheel reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by demand source with impressions, fill rate, revenue, and average CPM.
Check whether any guaranteed direct deal is under-delivering while a backfill partner tag is taking a large share of the same inventory.
Note any partner tag returning errors or near-zero fill, which can point to a stale endpoint or a format mismatch rather than thin demand.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per demand source, its priority tier, its budget or cap, and what reporting shows it delivering, alongside the publisher's account of which demand should win first. A setup where guaranteed direct demand sits above backfill partners, partner tags carry caps that protect committed delivery, and the tags themselves return fill passes. A setup where a guaranteed deal sits below a partner tag, an uncapped partner tag is crowding out under-delivering guaranteed demand, or a partner tag is returning errors is a fail recorded with the demand sources affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the priority, budget, and delivery for each demand source above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} runs demand tags whose priority or budget does not match what was sold. Priority decides which demand source gets first call on a video or Connected TV (CTV) impression, and budget caps how much each source can take, so a guaranteed direct deal placed below a backfill partner tag, or an uncapped partner tag competing against under-delivering guaranteed demand, trades committed revenue for whatever the auction returns. {{voice_recommend}} listing every direct and third-party demand source with its priority and budget, ordering them to match the publisher's delivery commitments, and capping the backfill partners so they cannot crowd out guaranteed demand. After the change, recheck fill and delivery by demand source to confirm the guaranteed deals are winning the impressions they were sold to fill.

audit_checks.title: Stale or non-performing demand tags are left running
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A demand tag from an ended deal or a partner that no longer returns fill still takes a turn in the auction, adding a call that returns nothing and delaying the impression's path to a paying buyer. The cost is small per impression, but across a high-volume video or Connected TV (CTV) setup it adds latency and obscures the reporting that should show which demand is actually earning its place.

educate.base:
Demand tags accumulate. A publisher adds a third-party partner for a campaign, a direct deal goes live for a quarter, a network tag gets bolted on to test fill, and over time the demand configuration in FreeWheel holds far more sources than are still earning their place. A tag from a deal that ended, or a partner that has stopped returning meaningful fill, does not remove itself. It stays in the configuration and keeps taking a turn in the auction for each video or CTV impression.

Each of those turns has a cost. A tag that no longer returns demand still gets called, waits for a response or a timeout, and then hands the impression on. In a video setup, where ad pods and player timeouts already constrain how long the impression can wait, a string of dead tags adds latency that can push the impression past the window the player allows, so an impression that a live tag could have filled goes empty instead. Stale tags also distort the picture a publisher reads since average fill and revenue per source are dragged down by tags that should not be counted at all.

The audit question is whether the demand configuration reflects the demand a publisher actually has now. A tag tied to an ended deal, a partner running well below the fill it once returned, or a test tag that was never cleaned up are all candidates for removal. Reporting is the evidence: a tag that has returned near-zero fill or no revenue across a meaningful window is doing nothing for the publisher except adding a call.

A few signs point to demand tags that have outlived their purpose:
  • A partner or direct tag tied to a deal whose flight dates have passed is still live in the configuration
  • A partner tag shows near-zero fill or no revenue across a recent reporting window while still being called on every impression
  • Test or trial tags added to evaluate a partner were never removed after the evaluation ended
  • The count of demand sources in the configuration is far higher than the number the publisher can name as currently active
You may want to confirm that every demand tag still corresponds to active demand. One approach is to pull fill and revenue by demand source across a recent window, then retire the tags that return nothing and the tags tied to deals that have already ended.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a streaming or broadcaster property, seasonal sponsorships and campaign flights come and go, so demand tags tied to a programming season or a one-off event can linger long after the flight closed. You may want to reconcile the live demand tags against the deals that are actually running now, retiring the tags whose flight dates have passed so they stop adding empty calls to the ad pod.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content video property, demand tags tied to a retail event or a limited brand partnership often have a defined run, so a tag left live past the campaign keeps competing for inventory it should no longer touch. You may want to confirm that demand tags tied to a finished retail campaign have been removed so they neither slow the auction nor distort the fill picture for the demand that remains.

educate.learn_more_note: FreeWheel's documentation for retiring or pausing demand sources is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the demand sources in the configuration against the deals that are active now and against fill and revenue from a recent reporting window, looking for tags that no longer correspond to live demand.

freewheel_ui.label: FreeWheel console
steps: Open the demand configuration and list every live direct-sold deal and third-party demand partner tag with its status and, where applicable, its flight dates.
Flag any tag whose flight dates have already passed and any tag the publisher cannot identify as currently active.
Note any test or trial tags that were added to evaluate a partner and never removed.

freewheel_reporting.label: FreeWheel reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by demand source with impressions, fill rate, and revenue across a recent window, for example the last 30 days.
Flag any demand source returning near-zero fill or no revenue across that window while still being called on each impression.
Cross-reference the flagged sources against the active-deal list to separate tags that are between flights from tags that should be retired.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records each demand source with its status, its flight dates where applicable, and its fill and revenue across a recent window. A configuration where every live tag corresponds to active demand and returns measurable fill passes. A configuration holding tags tied to ended deals, tags returning near-zero fill across the window, or test tags never cleaned up is a fail recorded with the specific tags that should be retired.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each demand source with its status, flight dates, and recent fill and revenue above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} leaves demand tags running after they have stopped earning their place. A tag from an ended deal or a partner that no longer returns fill still takes a turn in the auction for each video or Connected TV (CTV) impression, adding a call that returns nothing, and in a setup constrained by ad pods and player timeouts those empty calls add latency that can leave an impression unfilled. {{voice_recommend}} pulling fill and revenue by demand source across a recent window, retiring the tags tied to deals that have already ended and the tags returning near-zero fill, and removing any test tags that were never cleaned up. After the change, recheck the auction path so the demand that remains is the demand actually returning fill.
